title: Islamic Development Bank (IsDB) Director, Resilience & Climate Action Department – Lead Climate and Sustainable Development Across 57 Member Countries
summary: The Islamic Development Bank (IsDB) is seeking an experienced and visionary leader to serve as the Director, Resilience Climate Action Department , based at its headquarters in Jeddah, Saudi Arabia . This senior executive position offers a unique opportunity to shape climate action, resilience, disaster risk management, environmental sustainability, women and youth empowerment, and social development initiatives across the Bank s 57 Member Countries (MCs) . Reporting directly to the Director-General, Global Practices and Partnerships , the successful candidate will provide strategic leadership, oversee multidisciplinary teams, strengthen global partnerships, and guide the implementation of innovative development programmes that advance sustainable and resilient development. About the Role The Director will lead the Resilience Climate Action Department (RCA) , providing strategic oversight across several thematic areas critical to IsDB s development agenda. The department s work covers: Climate Change Environment and Social Safards Fragility, Conflict and Resilience (FCR) Disaster Risk Management Emergency Relief Civil Society and NGO Engagement Women s Empowerment Youth Development The Director will oversee the following divisions: Climate Change Environment Division Fragility Resilience Division Women Youth Empowerment Division Key Responsibilities Strategic Leadership The Director will: Develop and implement departmental strategies aligned with the IsDB Corporate Strategy. Lead annual and medium-term planning. Establish departmental priorities and performance indicators. Guide implementation of climate resilience and sustainability initiatives. Provide strategic advice to senior management. Policy Development Responsibilities include: Leading policy development across resilience and climate action. Supporting Member Country Partnership Strategies. Preparing policy briefs and strategic guidance. Monitoring global developments in sustainability and resilience. Integrating cross-cutting themes into Bank operations. Climate and Resilience Programming The successful candidate will oversee programmes focused on: Climate adaptation and mitigation. Environmental sustainability. Disaster preparedness. Emergency response. Fragility and conflict recovery. Capacity development. Women and youth inclusion. Civil society partnerships. Partnership Development The Director will strengthen relationships with: Governments. International development partners. NGOs. Civil society organizations. Multilateral institutions. Internal IsDB departments. The role also includes representing IsDB at international conferences and high-level forums. Leadership and People Management The successful candidate will: Lead multidisciplinary teams. Coach and mentor senior managers. Build organizational capability. Drive performance management. Foster collaboration across departments. Support recruitment and talent development. Operational Oversight The Director will also: Monitor departmental performance. Oversee budgets and resource allocation. Improve operational processes. Develop monitoring frameworks. Coordinate major Bank-wide resilience initiatives. Support sustainable finance programmes, including Sukuk initiatives. Required Skills The ideal candidate should demonstrate expertise in: Climate Change Environmental Sustainability Disaster Risk Management Fragility, Conflict and Resilience Women s Empowerment Youth Development Civil Society Engagement Sustainable Development Project Design Programme Management Policy Development Strategic Planning Partnership Building Economic Analysis Risk Management Organizational Leadership Strong leadership, integrity, stakeholder management, communication, and decision-making skills are essential. Educational Requirements Applicants must possess: A Master s degree or PhD in Environmental Sciences, Social Sciences, Conflict and Development, Social Development, Economics, or a related field. Professional Experience Candidates should have: At least 15 years of relevant professional experience. A minimum of 7 years in leadership or supervisory roles. At least 5 years working within a development institution. Proven experience leading large multidisciplinary teams. Demonstrated success delivering complex international development programmes. Why Join the Islamic Development Bank? Working with IsDB offers an opportunity to contribute to transformative development across 57 Member Countries , supporting projects that address climate resilience, sustainable development, poverty reduction, and social inclusion. As one of the world s leading multilateral development finance institutions, IsDB maintains a AAA credit rating and partners with governments, development agencies, and international organizations to deliver high-impact programmes aligned with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). This leadership role allows experienced professionals to influence policies, shape global partnerships, and deliver solutions that improve lives across Africa, Asia, Europe, Latin America, and the Middle East. Required Application Documents Applicants should prepare: Updated Resume/CV. Copy of Passport. Academic Certificates. Apply Here Application Deadline Closing Date: 30 July 2026 Applicants are encouraged to submit their applications before the deadline. Conclusion The Director, Resilience Climate Action Department position at the Islamic Development Bank is an outstanding opportunity for experienced international development leaders passionate about climate action, resilience, sustainable development, and inclusive growth. Leading strategic initiatives across 57 member countries, the successful candidate will play a critical role in advancing climate resilience, strengthening partnerships, and delivering impactful programmes that improve lives and support sustainable development worldwide. Professionals with extensive leadership experience in international development and climate-related sectors are encouraged to apply before 30 July 2026 . For more information on this opportunity, visit here Discover more job opportunities on OFY, visit here The post Islamic Development Bank (IsDB) Director, Resilience Climate Action Department – Lead Climate and Sustainable Development Across 57 Member Countries appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: World Bank GovTech Innovation Challenge 2026: Global Call for Proposals to Build Digital Solutions for Governments
summary: The World Bank , in partnership with the Swiss State Secretariat for Economic Affairs (SECO) and Trust Valley , has launched the GovTech Innovation Challenge 2026 , inviting innovative companies from around the world to develop cutting-edge digital solutions that transform public administration in emerging markets. This global Call for Proposals connects innovative businesses with government institutions seeking technology-driven solutions to improve public services, strengthen transparency, modernize public administration, and accelerate digital transformation. Selected innovators will participate in a fully funded programme that includes executive-level capacity building, a bootcamp in Switzerland, and the opportunity to work directly with government institutions to develop and demonstrate a Proof of Concept (PoC). About the GovTech Innovation Challenge Governments worldwide are increasingly embracing digital technologies to improve service delivery, strengthen governance, increase transparency, and enhance operational efficiency. The GovTech Innovation Challenge creates a bridge between innovative technology companies and public sector institutions by enabling businesses to co-create solutions that solve real government challenges. The programme is designed to support digital innovation in emerging markets while providing participating companies with valuable experience in delivering technology solutions to public sector clients. Programme Partners The initiative is jointly delivered by three globally recognized organizations: World Bank Swiss State Secretariat for Economic Affairs (SECO) Trust Valley Together, these organizations aim to accelerate public sector innovation through collaboration between governments and technology innovators. Benefits of Participating Selected companies gain access to a wide range of opportunities and support. 1. World Bank Partnership Participants benefit from: Exposure to public sector institutions. Credibility associated with World Bank-backed initiatives. Opportunities to engage with government stakeholders. Increased visibility within the international GovTech ecosystem. 2. Fully Funded Business-to-Government (B2G) Programme Successful applicants participate in an intensive capacity-building programme covering: Government procurement processes. Public sector collaboration. Regulatory compliance. Scaling GovTech businesses. Working with international development institutions. 3. Executive Bootcamp in Switzerland Top selected companies attend a bootcamp in Switzerland focused on: Public sector innovation. Business-to-government partnerships. International networking. Developing scalable GovTech solutions. 4. Opportunity to Build a Proof of Concept (PoC) Participants work directly with beneficiary government institutions to: Design digital solutions. Develop prototypes. Test solution feasibility. Demonstrate real-world impact. This hands-on collaboration provides valuable practical experience while helping governments solve pressing challenges. 5. Fully Covered Programme Costs The programme provides all core services at no cost . Selected participants receive support covering: Programme participation. Travel. Accommodation. Meals during official programme activities. How the Programme Works The GovTech Innovation Challenge follows a structured process. Step 1 – Submit a Proposal Applicants prepare and submit a proposal outlining: Their technical solution. Innovation approach. Expected impact. Ability to address a specific government challenge. Step 2 – Business-to-Government Capacity Building Top applicants join a specialized programme designed to strengthen their ability to work with governments. Participants receive: Executive training. Technical guidance. GovTech mentorship. International networking opportunities. Step 3 – Develop a Proof of Concept Selected companies collaborate directly with the beneficiary government to develop and demonstrate their proposed digital solution. Current Open GovTech Innovation Challenges The 2026 programme currently includes several innovation challenges from Morocco. Morocco – General Inspectorate of Finance (Inspection Générale des Finances – IGF) Challenge: Modernize: Audit mission programming. Public procurement auditing. Morocco – Supreme Audit Institution (Cour des Comptes – CDC) Challenge: Develop solutions that automate: Public procurement auditing. Compliance verification. Ethics monitoring. Transparency in public procurement. These challenges seek innovative digital technologies capable of improving accountability and public financial management. Previous Challenge Example The programme previously supported the Ghana Revenue Authority (GRA) challenge focused on: Improving tax resource mobilization. Strengthening digital tax administration. Enhancing government revenue systems. Although the Ghana challenge is now closed, it demonstrates the programme s commitment to solving practical public sector challenges across emerging economies. Why Companies Should Apply Participating organizations can: Expand into the government technology market. Validate innovative products. Collaborate with government institutions. Gain international recognition. Build partnerships with the World Bank ecosystem. Improve product credibility through real-world implementation. Access global GovTech networks. Who Is This Programme Best Suited For? The programme is ideal for organizations developing: Artificial Intelligence solutions. Digital audit systems. Public procurement platforms. Tax technologies. Data analytics tools. Automation software. Digital governance platforms. Regulatory technology solutions. Digital identity systems. Government management software. Application Process Interested companies should: Explore the available government challenges. Select a challenge aligned with their expertise. Prepare a proposal explaining their technical solution and expected impact. Submit the proposal through the official programme platform. Await evaluation and selection. Successful applicants will be invited to join the Business-to-Government Programme before working directly with government partners on developing their Proof of Concept. Apply Here Application Deadline The programme features multiple open calls , each with its own submission timeline. Applicants should review the specific challenge page for the applicable deadline before submitting their proposal. Conclusion The World Bank GovTech Innovation Challenge 2026 provides an outstanding opportunity for innovative companies to collaborate with governments, develop impactful digital solutions, and gain international exposure. Through funding support, executive training, direct government engagement, and fully covered programme participation, the initiative enables innovators to transform public administration while expanding their presence in the global GovTech ecosystem. Technology companies seeking to create meaningful public sector impact are encouraged to submit proposals for one of the current open challenges. For more information on this opportunity, visit here Discover more global opportunities on OFY, here The post World Bank GovTech Innovation Challenge 2026: Global Call for Proposals to Build Digital Solutions for Governments appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: World Sustainability Award 2027: $100,000 Prize Honouring Groundbreaking Research and Transformative Contributions to Sustainability
summary: The World Sustainability Award 2027 , presented by the MDPI Sustainability Foundation , is a prestigious international recognition celebrating researchers whose groundbreaking work has contributed significantly to advancing sustainability and creating positive global impact. The award recognizes exceptional scholars whose research has demonstrated transformative contributions to sustainability challenges, whether through broad sustainability innovation or through solutions addressing specific sustainability-related issues. The selected recipient will receive a USD $100,000 monetary prize , recognizing outstanding academic excellence, scientific innovation, and societal impact in sustainability research. The award will be officially presented during the World Sustainability Forum 2027 , bringing together global experts, researchers, policymakers, and sustainability leaders committed to addressing pressing environmental and societal challenges. About the World Sustainability Award The World Sustainability Award celebrates individuals who are shaping the future of sustainability through research, discovery, and innovation. Sustainability challenges today require interdisciplinary approaches that combine science, technology, policy, and social innovation. Through this award, the MDPI Sustainability Foundation recognizes researchers whose work provides meaningful solutions and contributes to long-term global sustainability. The award highlights researchers who have demonstrated excellence in areas such as: Developing innovative sustainability solutions. Advancing scientific understanding of environmental challenges. Creating technologies that support sustainable development. Influencing sustainable practices and policies. Addressing complex global challenges through research and collaboration. Award Prize and Recognition The winner of the 2027 World Sustainability Award will receive: USD $100,000 Research Recognition Prize The monetary award recognizes the recipient’s outstanding achievements and contributions to sustainability research. The prize represents: International recognition of scholarly excellence. Appreciation for impactful sustainability research. Support for continued advancement of sustainability knowledge. Recognition among leading global sustainability experts. Who Should Be Considered for the Award? The award is designed for researchers whose work has achieved exceptional impact in sustainability. Potential nominees may include researchers who have: Produced influential sustainability research. Developed innovative approaches to environmental or social challenges. Created solutions with measurable global or community-level impact. Advanced sustainability knowledge across disciplines. Contributed to improving the relationship between human development and environmental protection. The award welcomes contributions from diverse sustainability fields, recognizing that meaningful solutions often emerge from collaboration across scientific disciplines. Areas of Research and Impact Recognized The World Sustainability Award may recognize research contributions related to: 1. Environmental Sustainability Including work focused on: Climate change mitigation and adaptation. Ecosystem protection. Biodiversity conservation. Environmental restoration. Sustainable resource management. 2. Sustainable Technologies and Innovation Including: Renewable energy solutions. Green technologies. Sustainable materials. Low-carbon innovations. Technologies supporting environmental protection. 3. Sustainable Development Including research addressing: Sustainable cities. Responsible production and consumption. Social sustainability. Economic resilience. Inclusive development approaches. 4. Food and Agricultural Sustainability Including contributions related to: Sustainable farming systems. Climate-resilient agriculture. Food security. Sustainable supply chains. Responsible use of natural resources. Nomination Process for the World Sustainability Award 2027 The nomination process will officially open on: Wednesday, 1 July 2026 Candidates must be nominated by a qualified third party through the official online nomination platform. The nomination process includes: Submission of a nomination by an eligible third party. Review of submitted nominations based on the award criteria. Assessment by an international Selection Committee. Selection of the award recipient. Official announcement and award presentation during the World Sustainability Forum 2027. Important Nomination Requirement Unlike some awards, the World Sustainability Award does not accept direct applications from researchers. Researchers interested in being considered must: Be nominated by another qualified individual or organization. Ensure their achievements and research impact are clearly presented. Provide evidence demonstrating the significance of their sustainability contributions. Selection Process After the nomination deadline, all eligible submissions will be reviewed by the Selection Committee. The committee consists of internationally recognized sustainability experts who evaluate nominees based on: Research excellence. Originality and innovation. Scientific contribution. Societal impact. Contribution to advancing sustainability goals. Long-term influence of the researcher’s work. The committee will select one outstanding researcher as the recipient of the 2027 award. Award Ceremony The selected recipient is expected to attend the official award ceremony held during the: World Sustainability Forum 2027 The ceremony will provide an opportunity to celebrate sustainability leadership and connect the award recipient with global experts working toward sustainable solutions. Why the World Sustainability Award Matters Sustainability challenges such as climate change, resource depletion, environmental degradation, and social inequality require innovative research and global collaboration. By recognizing outstanding researchers, the World Sustainability Award helps: Promote scientific excellence in sustainability. Encourage groundbreaking research. Highlight solutions with global impact. Inspire future generations of sustainability researchers. Strengthen collaboration among sustainability leaders worldwide. Timeline and Deadline Information Nomination Opens: Wednesday, 1 July 2026 Nomination Deadline: The official closing date for nominations will be announced through the award’s nomination guidelines. Award Presentation: During the World Sustainability Forum 2027. How to Participate Researchers, institutions, academic leaders, and sustainability professionals who know exceptional sustainability researchers are encouraged to prepare nominations. Nomination steps include: Identify an eligible researcher with outstanding sustainability contributions. Prepare supporting information highlighting their achievements and impact. Submit the nomination through the official online platform. Await review by the international Selection Committee. Apply Here Conclusion The World Sustainability Award 2027 represents one of the leading global recognitions for researchers creating meaningful solutions to sustainability challenges. With a USD $100,000 prize , international recognition, and presentation at the World Sustainability Forum 2027, the award provides an opportunity to celebrate scientists and scholars whose research is helping build a more sustainable future. Researchers whose work has transformed sustainability knowledge, influenced society, or delivered innovative solutions are encouraged to be nominated for this prestigious global award. For more information on this award, visit here Discover more global award opportunities on OFY, here The post World Sustainability Award 2027: $100,000 Prize Honouring Groundbreaking Research and Transformative Contributions to Sustainability appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: 51 Pegasi b Fellowship 2026/2027: Up to $492,000 Research Support for Postdoctoral Scientists Advancing Planetary Astronomy and Exoplanet Discovery
summary: The Heising-Simons Foundation 51 Pegasi b Fellowship 2026/2027 is now accepting applications from outstanding postdoctoral scientists seeking to advance groundbreaking research in planetary astronomy. This prestigious fellowship provides exceptional researchers with the opportunity to establish themselves as independent leaders while exploring some of the most important questions about planets, planetary systems, and worlds beyond our solar system. Established in 2017, the fellowship is named after 51 Pegasi b , the first confirmed exoplanet discovered orbiting a Sun-like star. The discovery transformed modern astronomy by opening a new era of research into planets beyond our solar system and how planetary systems form, evolve, and potentially support life. The fellowship supports scientists whose research contributes to expanding humanity’s understanding of planetary environments, astronomical technologies, and the processes that shape planets both within and beyond our solar system. About the 51 Pegasi b Fellowship The 51 Pegasi b Fellowship is designed to support emerging leaders in planetary astronomy by providing funding, mentorship, networking opportunities, and the independence needed to conduct ambitious scientific research. The fellowship supports research across multiple areas, including: Planet formation and evolution. Exoplanet discovery and characterization. Planetary system dynamics. Astronomical observation techniques. Development of technologies for detecting distant worlds. Theoretical modeling of planetary environments. Experimental approaches that improve understanding of planets and their systems. Through this fellowship, researchers gain the resources needed to pursue innovative ideas that can create significant advancements in planetary science and astronomy. What the Fellowship Provides Successful fellows receive comprehensive support designed to accelerate their scientific careers and research impact. 1. Up to $492,000 in Research Funding Selected fellows receive up to: $492,000 in financial support over three years . The possibility of applying for an additional fourth year of funding, subject to satisfactory research progress. This funding enables fellows to dedicate time and resources toward independent research projects, including scientific investigations, data analysis, technological development, and collaboration. 2. Opportunity to Establish Scientific Leadership The fellowship provides researchers with: Time to focus on high-impact scientific questions. The independence required to develop their own research identity. The opportunity to build recognition as emerging leaders in planetary astronomy. Support to pursue innovative and potentially transformative research directions. 3. Mentorship From Leading Scientists Each fellow receives guidance and mentorship from an established faculty member at their host institution. Mentorship support helps fellows: Strengthen their research strategy. Build professional networks. Develop leadership skills. Navigate career advancement opportunities in academia and scientific research. 4. Annual Fellowship Summit and Networking Opportunities Fellows participate in an annual summit designed to encourage: Professional networking. Scientific collaboration. Exchange of research ideas. Knowledge sharing among leading researchers. Development of long-term partnerships within the planetary astronomy community. The summit creates a platform for fellows to connect with experts and other researchers working on innovative discoveries. Why the 51 Pegasi b Fellowship Matters Planetary astronomy is one of the fastest-growing areas of modern science. Researchers in this field are helping answer fundamental questions about: How planets are created. Why planetary systems develop differently. How planets interact with their environments. Whether other worlds may contain conditions suitable for life. How new technologies can improve the search for distant planets. By supporting talented scientists, the fellowship contributes to discoveries that expand knowledge of the universe and strengthen the future of astronomy. Commitment to Diversity and Inclusion The Heising-Simons Foundation encourages applications from researchers with diverse backgrounds and perspectives. The fellowship particularly welcomes scientists whose experiences, identities, or career paths are underrepresented in planetary astronomy and who bring innovative ideas capable of creating meaningful contributions to the field. The foundation believes that diverse scientific communities strengthen discovery, creativity, and collaboration. Application Information for the 51 Pegasi b Fellowship 2026/2027 The application period for the 2026/2027 51 Pegasi b Fellowship is currently open. Applicants are required to submit: An online application form. Two letters of recommendation. Supporting materials required under the official application guidelines. Applicants should carefully review the eligibility requirements, evaluation criteria, and submission instructions before applying. Deadline for Application Application Deadline: October 1, 2026, at 2:00 PM Pacific Time Applications submitted after the deadline may not be considered. How Applications Are Evaluated Applicants are assessed based on factors including: The quality and originality of their proposed research. Potential contribution to planetary astronomy. Scientific creativity and innovation. Research experience and achievements. Ability to conduct independent research. Potential for leadership and long-term impact in the field. Research Areas Supported by the Fellowship The fellowship supports scientists working in areas such as: Exoplanet science. Planetary formation and evolution. Stellar and planetary systems. Astronomical instrumentation. Observational astronomy. Computational planetary science. Experimental planetary research. Methods for detecting and studying distant worlds. How to Apply Interested researchers should: Review the official fellowship eligibility requirements. Prepare their research proposal and supporting documents. Secure required recommendation letters. Complete and submit the online application before the deadline. Monitor communication from the fellowship team regarding application updates. For questions and additional information, applicants may contact the Heising-Simons Foundation science team at science@hsfoundation.org . Apply Here Conclusion The 51 Pegasi b Fellowship represents a significant opportunity for postdoctoral scientists passionate about exploring planets, exoplanets, and the mysteries of planetary systems. With substantial research funding, expert mentorship, and a global scientific network, the fellowship provides emerging researchers with the platform needed to make meaningful contributions to planetary astronomy and shape the future of space science. Researchers interested in advancing humanity’s understanding of worlds beyond Earth are encouraged to apply before the October 1, 2026 deadline. For more information on this fellowship opportunity, visit here For more fellowship opportunities on OFY, click here The post 51 Pegasi b Fellowship 2026/2027: Up to $492,000 Research Support for Postdoctoral Scientists Advancing Planetary Astronomy and Exoplanet Discovery appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: NCWorks Workforce Pell Grant Program Opens Applications for Eligible Short-Term Workforce Training Providers in North Carolina
summary: The NCWorks Commission , in partnership with Governor Josh Stein and the North Carolina Department of Commerce , has officially opened applications for eligible training providers seeking approval to participate in the new Workforce Pell Grant Program . This landmark initiative expands access to federal Pell Grant financial aid for students enrolled in approved short-term workforce training programs. By making federal funding available for high-quality, career-focused education, the program aims to strengthen North Carolina s workforce while helping residents acquire in-demand skills for today s labor market. The Workforce Pell initiative represents a significant advancement in workforce development by creating new pathways to industry-recognized credentials, certificates, and career opportunities in high-demand sectors across the state. Application Timeline Applications for eligible training providers are now open. Institutions interested in participating should begin the application process as soon as possible. The NCWorks Commission reviews Workforce Pell applications quarterly. What Is the Workforce Pell Grant Program? The Workforce Pell Grant Program expands eligibility for Federal Pell Grants to students enrolled in approved short-term workforce education programs. Traditionally, Pell Grants have primarily supported students pursuing longer academic programs. Under Workforce Pell, eligible learners can now receive federal financial aid while completing shorter, skills-based training programs designed to prepare them for immediate employment. The initiative helps reduce financial barriers for students while providing employers with a stronger pipeline of skilled workers. Governor Josh Stein emphasized that as North Carolina continues attracting record levels of business investment and job creation, Workforce Pell will ensure residents are prepared to take advantage of these opportunities. Why Workforce Pell Matters North Carolina continues to experience unprecedented economic growth. Since taking office in January 2025, Governor Josh Stein has announced: More than 42,000 new jobs Over $29 billion in new business investments Recognition as the Top State for Workforce Development by Site Selection Magazine in January 2026 The Workforce Pell Grant Program directly supports these economic achievements by expanding access to workforce education and helping students gain industry-valued skills. Objectives of the Program The Workforce Pell initiative seeks to: Expand access to affordable workforce education Increase enrollment in short-term career training Address employer workforce shortages Support high-demand industries Strengthen North Carolina s talent pipeline Increase access to industry-recognized credentials Improve career pathways for residents Support economic competitiveness Who Can Apply? Applications are currently being accepted from eligible education and training providers , not individual students. Institutions wishing to participate must first determine whether their workforce training programs satisfy federal Workforce Pell requirements. Workforce Pell Eligibility Requirements Eligible training programs must satisfy several federal criteria, including: Last at least eight weeks Last fewer than fifteen weeks Align with high-skill, high-wage, or in-demand industries Count toward academic credit Lead to a certificate or degree pathway Meet all U.S. Department of Education Workforce Pell requirements Programs meeting these criteria may proceed through the North Carolina state approval process. Application Process Eligible institutions should follow these steps: Step 1 Review each workforce training program to confirm compliance with federal Workforce Pell requirements. Step 2 Complete the Eligible Training Provider (ETP) application. Step 3 Submit the additional documentation required for Workforce Pell approval. Step 4 The NCWorks Commission conducts quarterly reviews of submitted applications. Step 5 Upon receiving state approval, institutions may submit their eligible programs to the U.S. Department of Education for final federal approval. Apply Here State Approval Process The NCWorks Commission serves as North Carolina s lead agency for Workforce Pell implementation. The Commission adopted the state s Workforce Pell approval policy in May 2026 following the release of final federal regulations by the U.S. Department of Education. The review process ensures participating institutions meet both federal and state quality standards. Governor s Investment in Workforce Development Governor Josh Stein has made workforce development a central priority of his administration. To support implementation of Workforce Pell, the Governor s proposed 2026–2027 budget includes more than $144,000 to establish a dedicated Workforce Pell Director within the North Carolina Department of Commerce. If approved by the North Carolina General Assembly, the Workforce Pell Director will: Coordinate statewide implementation Support institutional approval processes Collaborate with workforce partners Expand access to approved workforce training programs Key Partners Development of North Carolina s Workforce Pell policy involved collaboration among numerous education and workforce stakeholders through the Governor s Council on Workforce and Apprenticeships. Key partners include: NCWorks Commission North Carolina Department of Commerce North Carolina Community College System University of North Carolina System North Carolina Independent Colleges and Universities Governor s Council on Workforce and Apprenticeships U.S. Department of Education Supporting North Carolina s Economic Development Strategy Workforce Pell also advances the state s broader First in Opportunity Strategic Economic Development Plan , which emphasizes: Building a resilient workforce Preparing workers for future industries Expanding access to valuable credentials Strengthening employment pathways Supporting employer workforce needs Funding The NCWorks Commission is funded through the Workforce Innovation and Opportunity Act (WIOA) Title I , supported entirely by the Employment and Training Administration of the U.S. Department of Labor , totaling $66,329,591 . Benefits of Workforce Pell Participating institutions and future students stand to benefit through: Expanded federal financial aid access Increased workforce training enrollment Stronger employer partnerships Improved workforce readiness Support for high-demand industries Enhanced credential attainment Greater career opportunities Increased economic mobility Why Eligible Institutions Should Apply Workforce Pell creates an unprecedented opportunity for colleges and workforce training providers to offer federally funded short-term education that aligns directly with employer needs. By becoming an approved Workforce Pell provider, institutions can attract more students, expand access to career-focused education, and contribute to North Carolina s rapidly growing economy. For more information on this grant visit here For more grant opportunities on OFY, click here The post NCWorks Workforce Pell Grant Program Opens Applications for Eligible Short-Term Workforce Training Providers in North Carolina appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: CSC Alumni Community Engagement Fund (ACEF) 2026-27: Funding Opportunity for Commonwealth Alumni to Create Community Impact
summary: Commonwealth Alumni with innovative ideas for community development projects can now apply for the Alumni Community Engagement Fund (ACEF) 2026-27 , an annual funding opportunity designed to support alumni-led activities that promote knowledge sharing, awareness creation, and practical solutions to development challenges. The fund supports Commonwealth Alumni who want to transform ideas into meaningful community action through workshops, training sessions, community forums, ideathons, civic engagement projects, and other collaborative activities. The Commonwealth Scholarship Commission (CSC) created ACEF to recognise that lasting change often begins at the community level. By supporting alumni-led initiatives, the programme enables individuals and groups to share knowledge, build networks, and encourage practical action on important global development issues. The application deadline is: Sunday, 26 July 2026 at 23:59 BST About the CSC Alumni Community Engagement Fund (ACEF) The Alumni Community Engagement Fund is an annual initiative of the Commonwealth Scholarship Commission designed to support Commonwealth Alumni in creating community-based engagement activities. The programme focuses on empowering alumni to become active contributors to development by using their skills, knowledge, and networks to address challenges within their communities. ACEF provides financial support for activities that encourage: Awareness raising Knowledge exchange Community dialogue Skills development Collaborative problem-solving Practical action The fund recognises that small, locally driven initiatives can create significant long-term impact when communities are equipped with knowledge, resources, and opportunities to work together. Purpose of the ACEF Programme The main purpose of ACEF is to help Commonwealth Alumni design and deliver activities that respond to important development issues while strengthening community participation. The programme encourages alumni to move beyond discussions and create practical solutions that benefit their communities. Supported activities should aim to: Educate communities about key development challenges. Encourage conversations and collaboration. Share professional and academic knowledge. Build community capacity. Inspire individuals and organisations to take action. 2026-27 ACEF Priority Themes For the 2026-27 funding cycle, applicants are invited to submit projects addressing one of three priority themes. Applicants must choose only one theme when applying. 1. Artificial Intelligence for Development This theme supports initiatives exploring how artificial intelligence can contribute to sustainable development and community progress. Potential activities may focus on: Raising awareness about AI opportunities and challenges. Building digital skills. Exploring responsible AI use. Encouraging innovation through technology. Helping communities understand how AI can support development goals. 2. Strengthening Blue Economies in Small Island Developing States (SIDS) This theme focuses on supporting sustainable economic opportunities connected to marine and coastal resources. Projects may explore: Sustainable use of ocean resources. Coastal community development. Marine conservation. Innovation within blue economies. Climate resilience for island communities. Important eligibility requirement: Applicants applying under this theme must be living or working in a Commonwealth Small Island Developing State (SIDS). 3. Women Entrepreneurs: Catalyst for Change This theme focuses on supporting women entrepreneurs and recognising their role in economic and social transformation. Activities may address: Entrepreneurship skills development. Business knowledge sharing. Women’s economic empowerment. Innovation and leadership. Creating supportive networks for women entrepreneurs. Types of Activities ACEF Can Support Applicants can propose a wide range of community engagement activities. Examples include: Skills-Based Training Projects may include: Masterclasses Training workshops Professional development sessions Capacity-building activities Educational Events Examples include: Lectures Seminars Knowledge-sharing sessions Community learning events Civic Engagement Projects Applicants may design activities that: Encourage community participation. Promote awareness of development issues. Support collective action. Community Forums Activities may involve: Public discussions Dialogue sessions Stakeholder meetings Community consultations Ideathons and Innovation Events Applicants may organise: Problem-solving competitions Innovation challenges Collaborative idea-generation sessions Benefits of Participating in ACEF ACEF provides more than financial support. It gives Commonwealth Alumni the opportunity to strengthen their leadership and community engagement skills. Benefits include: Community Impact Participants can: Address important local challenges. Support community development. Create lasting positive change. Professional Development Organisers can strengthen skills in: Project management Public speaking Event delivery Community engagement Leadership Networking Opportunities ACEF enables alumni to: Expand professional networks. Connect with other Commonwealth Alumni. Share knowledge and experiences. Who Can Apply? ACEF applications are open to: Individual Commonwealth Alumni Individuals who have benefited from Commonwealth education programmes can apply to deliver community engagement activities. Commonwealth Alumni Associations Recognised alumni associations can submit proposals for community projects. Groups of Commonwealth Alumni Groups of alumni who are not part of an existing alumni association can also apply. Application Requirements and Guidelines Applicants should ensure their proposals: Clearly explain the community need being addressed. Identify the intended audience. Describe the proposed activity. Explain how the activity will create lasting impact. Demonstrate how knowledge-sharing and engagement will take place. Applicants must: Submit only one application. Apply under only one priority theme. Review the ACEF Terms and Conditions before applying. How to Prepare a Strong Application Applicants are encouraged to: Understand the Needs of Their Community Strong proposals should clearly explain: Who will benefit from the activity. Why the activity is needed. How it responds to community challenges. Design Practical and Meaningful Activities Successful projects should include: Clear objectives. Realistic activities. Expected outcomes. Plans for sustainability. Demonstrate Long-Term Impact Applicants should explain how their activity can continue creating value beyond the funding period. Information Sessions for Applicants The CSC will host information sessions to help applicants understand the programme and prepare stronger applications. Sessions will cover: Programme overview 2026-27 themes Application guidance Practical tips for developing proposals Information session dates: Session 1 Tuesday, 14 July 2026 Time: 12:00 BST Session 2 Wednesday, 15 July 2026 Time: 21:00 BST Both sessions provide the same information, so applicants only need to attend one. Registration must be completed by: 11:00 AM BST on Monday, 13 July 2026 Application Deadline Sunday, 26 July 2026 at 23:59 BST Applications submitted after the deadline will not be considered. The CSC does not provide feedback on unsuccessful applications. How to Apply Before submitting an application, applicants should: Read the ACEF 2026-27 Terms and Conditions. Review the application guidance. Prepare responses using the application form. Submit the online application before the deadline. Click here for more and to apply Why Commonwealth Alumni Should Apply The ACEF programme provides Commonwealth Alumni with a platform to turn knowledge into action. By supporting alumni-led projects, the fund helps communities address development challenges through collaboration, innovation, and shared learning. For alumni who want to create meaningful change, ACEF offers an opportunity to transform ideas into practical initiatives that can benefit communities for years to come. Click here for more global grant opportunities The post CSC Alumni Community Engagement Fund (ACEF) 2026-27: Funding Opportunity for Commonwealth Alumni to Create Community Impact appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: IKEA Foundation Hiring Head of Learning & Impact in the Netherlands: Lead Global Learning, Strategy, and Social Impact Initiatives
summary: Professionals passionate about social impact, organisational learning, philanthropy, and global change have an exciting career opportunity to join the IKEA Foundation as Head of Learning Impact . The IKEA Foundation is seeking an experienced, collaborative, and purpose-driven leader who can guide the organisation’s learning and impact agenda by developing systems, practices, and a culture that enable meaningful learning across the Foundation and its global partnerships. This full-time leadership role is based at the IKEA Foundation office in Leiden, the Netherlands , and offers an opportunity to influence how one of the world’s major philanthropic organisations understands impact, improves decision-making, and supports long-term global transformation. The application deadline is: Monday, 10 August 2026 at 10:00 AM CEST About the IKEA Foundation The IKEA Foundation is an independent philanthropic organisation dedicated to fighting global warming and supporting solutions that improve people’s lives. Although the Foundation shares a heritage and values connection with IKEA, it operates independently from the IKEA business. The organisation focuses on supporting partners working toward long-term changes in high-emitting systems that affect communities worldwide. The Foundation’s mission centres on creating a better everyday life for people by supporting initiatives that address major global challenges. Its work focuses on four key areas: Energy Land, food, and forests Buildings Transport Through partnerships and philanthropic funding, the IKEA Foundation supports people-centred solutions aimed at reducing and avoiding greenhouse gas emissions while creating sustainable social and environmental impact. To date, the Foundation has provided nearly €3 billion in funding to partners around the world. About the Head of Learning Impact Role The Head of Learning Impact will lead the Foundation’s Learning and Impact team and oversee the development of approaches that help the organisation understand: What is working Why it is working What lessons can guide future decisions How partnerships and strategies can continuously improve The role is not focused only on measuring outcomes through traditional indicators. Instead, it requires someone who understands that social and environmental systems are complex, constantly changing, and influenced by many factors. The successful candidate will help create an environment where evidence, reflection, experience, and insights inform strategic choices. Key Responsibilities The Head of Learning Impact will be responsible for leading and strengthening the Foundation’s learning and impact approach. Major responsibilities include: Leading the Learning and Impact Strategy The successful candidate will: Develop and guide the Foundation’s learning and impact strategy. Ensure learning is integrated into organisational decision-making. Build systems that allow teams to generate and use meaningful insights. Support continuous improvement across programmes and partnerships. Building a Culture of Organisational Learning The role will help create a workplace culture where learning is shared and valued. Responsibilities include: Encouraging reflection and dialogue. Supporting teams to learn from successes and challenges. Creating safe environments for innovation and adaptation. Helping colleagues connect evidence with strategic decisions. Managing and Developing the Learning Impact Team The successful candidate will act as a strong people leader by: Providing direction and support to the team. Strengthening team culture. Creating conditions for high-quality work. Encouraging collaboration, curiosity, and professional growth. Supporting Evidence-Based Decision Making The Head of Learning Impact will ensure that: Data and insights inform organisational choices. Lessons from programmes influence future strategies. Teams understand the impact of their work. Evidence is translated into practical action. Collaborating Across the Organisation The role requires close cooperation with teams including: Programmes Convening and Engagement Communications Operations People and Culture The successful candidate will help embed learning as a shared organisational responsibility. Ideal Candidate Profile The IKEA Foundation is looking for someone who can combine strategic thinking with practical leadership. The ideal candidate will demonstrate: Leadership Experience Candidates should have experience: Leading teams or organisational functions. Managing learning, evaluation, impact, or strategy-related work. Supporting organisations through change and complexity. Understanding of Complex Systems The successful applicant should understand: Systems change approaches. Adaptive learning. Social impact measurement. The challenges of creating change in complex environments. They should be able to bring structure while remaining flexible and open to emerging opportunities. Strong Communication and Collaboration Skills The role requires someone who can: Facilitate meaningful conversations. Build relationships across different teams and organisations. Communicate complex ideas clearly. Encourage collaboration among diverse stakeholders. Workplace Culture and Values The IKEA Foundation identifies togetherness as a central value of its work. The organisation believes that global challenges cannot be solved by one organisation alone and requires collaboration between communities, organisations, governments, and partners. The successful candidate will be expected to promote: Collaboration Inclusion Psychological safety Curiosity Continuous learning Shared responsibility Job Location and Employment Details Position: Head, Learning Impact Organisation: IKEA Foundation Location: Leiden, Netherlands Employment type: Full-time (38 hours per week) Additional requirements: International travel may be required. Fluency in English, written and spoken, is essential. Salary and benefits: Competitive compensation package. Application Requirements Applicants should submit: 1. Cover Letter The cover letter should explain: Motivation for applying Relevant qualifications Leadership experience Interest in the IKEA Foundation’s mission 2. Curriculum Vitae (CV) Applicants should provide a complete professional CV highlighting relevant experience. How to Apply Applications should be submitted through the Perrett Laver recruitment portal. Click here for more and to apply Applicants should quote reference number: 8497 Application Deadline The closing date for applications is: Monday, 10 August 2026 at 10:00 AM CEST Interested candidates are encouraged to prepare their applications early and clearly demonstrate how their experience aligns with organisational learning, impact strategy, and global social change. Why This Opportunity Matters The Head of Learning Impact role provides a unique opportunity for an experienced professional to influence how a global philanthropic organisation learns, adapts, and creates lasting impact. By shaping learning systems and strengthening evidence-based decision-making, the successful candidate will help ensure that resources, partnerships, and strategies contribute effectively to addressing some of the world’s most urgent challenges. title: Feminist Leadership Accelerator for Change (FLAC) Fellowship 2026: A 12-Month Leadership Programme Empowering Young African Women to Drive Social Change
summary: Young African women passionate about equality, justice, activism, and community transformation are invited to apply for the Feminist Leadership Accelerator for Change (FLAC) Fellowship 2026 , a 12-month leadership development programme designed to equip emerging feminist leaders with the skills, knowledge, confidence, and networks needed to create meaningful social change. The fellowship, organised by the Soul City Institute for Social Justice (SCI), provides a transformative learning and collaboration space where young women can develop feminist consciousness, strengthen leadership abilities, and engage in practical community action. Applications are open for Cohort 3 of the FLAC Fellowship , with the deadline set for: 27 July 2026 The programme welcomes young women who envision a more just and equitable society and who are committed to challenging harmful systems, advancing women’s rights, and building stronger communities. About the Feminist Leadership Accelerator for Change (FLAC) Fellowship The Feminist Leadership Accelerator for Change (FLAC) is a flagship fellowship programme created to nurture the next generation of African feminist leaders. The fellowship focuses on developing young women who are prepared to question inequality, challenge patriarchy, promote human rights, and lead community-based solutions. FLAC provides a safe and collaborative environment where fellows can: Develop feminist awareness and leadership skills Learn from experienced activists and changemakers Engage in meaningful conversations about social justice Build confidence to lead community initiatives Connect with a network of like-minded young women Transform ideas into practical action The programme encourages participants to embrace lifelong learning, feminist activism, and leadership rooted in dignity, equality, and justice. Through this fellowship, participants are supported to become leaders who can influence positive change within their communities and beyond. Programme Duration and Structure The FLAC Fellowship is a 12-month leadership programme combining: Immersive learning sessions Peer discussions Leadership development activities Practical community projects Professional development support Throughout the fellowship, participants explore key themes related to feminism, leadership, advocacy, justice, and community transformation. FLAC Fellowship Modules and Learning Areas The programme covers a wide range of topics designed to strengthen participants’ leadership capacity. 1. Gender and Healing: Childhood Conditioning and Recovery Participants explore how personal experiences, social expectations, and gender norms influence identity and leadership. This module focuses on: Understanding gender conditioning Personal healing journeys Building self-awareness Developing healthier approaches to leadership 2. Feminism 101: African Feminist Epistemology and Decolonial Thought This module introduces participants to feminist theories and African feminist perspectives. Participants learn about: The history and principles of feminism African feminist movements Decolonial approaches to knowledge and leadership Systems that influence gender inequality 3. Communications and Media Part A: Photo-Voice and Feminist Storytelling Participants develop communication skills to share stories and advocate for social change. Learning areas include: Storytelling techniques Using personal and community experiences for advocacy Visual communication approaches Amplifying underrepresented voices 4. Feminist Activism and Movement Building This module focuses on creating collective action and sustainable movements. Participants explore: Activism strategies Community organising Building networks for change Strengthening feminist movements 5. Sexual and Gender-Based Violence (SGBV) and Sexual and Reproductive Justice Participants examine issues affecting women and girls, including: Sexual and gender-based violence Reproductive rights Access to justice Creating safer communities 6. Feminist Leadership: Power, Ethics, and Practice This module develops leadership approaches based on: Ethical decision-making Understanding power dynamics Inclusive leadership Responsible community engagement 7. Economic and Climate Justice Through a Feminist Lens Participants explore how economic inequality and climate challenges affect women differently. Topics include: Economic empowerment Environmental justice Gender-responsive solutions Sustainable development 8. Communications and Media Part B: Advocacy Campaigns and Digital Strategy Participants learn how to use communication tools for social impact. Skills include: Designing advocacy campaigns Digital engagement strategies Online storytelling Public awareness initiatives 9. Community Accelerator Project (CAP): Design and Implementation Fellows develop and implement community projects addressing real challenges. Participants learn: Project design Implementation planning Community engagement Measuring impact 10. Job Readiness and Professional Development The fellowship also prepares participants for future career opportunities through: Professional skills development Career preparation Workplace readiness Leadership growth Benefits of the FLAC Fellowship Selected fellows will gain: A deeper understanding of feminist leadership principles Skills to lead community transformation projects Knowledge of social justice movements Confidence in advocacy and public engagement Access to a network of young African women leaders Practical experience developing community initiatives Professional development support The programme provides participants with both personal growth and practical tools for creating lasting impact. Eligibility Requirements Applicants must meet all eligibility requirements below. Age Requirement Applicants must be: Between 19 and 25 years old at the time of application. Gender Identity Requirement Applicants must: Self-identify as women. The programme recognises women based on self-identification and welcomes all individuals who identify as women. Educational Requirement Applicants must have at least: A high school certificate, Diploma, or Undergraduate degree. Leadership and Social Justice Commitment Applicants should demonstrate: Interest in feminist leadership Commitment to social justice Passion for community development Engagement in activism or leadership activities Experience supporting women’s rights initiatives is preferred. Eligible Countries The FLAC Fellowship is open to young women living in: South Africa Lesotho Eswatini Botswana Zimbabwe Young women from rural communities and applicants with disabilities are particularly encouraged to apply. Required Application Documents Applicants must submit the following materials: 1. Curriculum Vitae (CV) The CV should: Be no longer than three pages Highlight leadership experiences Include an example of when the applicant demonstrated leadership in their community or personal life 2. Motivation Letter Applicants must explain: Why they want to join FLAC Their interest in feminist leadership Their activism or community involvement The change they hope to create 3. Motivation Video Applicants must submit a short video: Duration: 2–3 minutes Explaining their passion for social justice Describing the change they want to contribute to 4. Two Recommendation Letters Applicants must provide two letters from community representatives who can discuss: Work ethic Leadership abilities Commitment to community work The recommenders must include: Name Position Contact information Relationship with the applicant Friends and family members cannot provide these recommendations. 5. Reference Letter From Educational Institution Applicants must submit a reference letter from: A former high school educator, or A tertiary lecturer The letter must confirm the applicant’s active leadership role during their studies. How to Apply Interested applicants must complete the online application process and submit all required documents before the deadline. Click here for more and to apply Application Deadline 27 July 2026 Applicants are encouraged to prepare their documents early to ensure they submit a complete application package before the closing date. Why Young Women Should Apply The FLAC Fellowship offers an opportunity for young African women to strengthen their leadership abilities, connect with other changemakers, and develop practical solutions to social challenges. For young women committed to equality, justice, and community transformation, this fellowship provides a platform to learn, collaborate, and lead meaningful change. Click here for more global fellowship opportunities The post Feminist Leadership Accelerator for Change (FLAC) Fellowship 2026: A 12-Month Leadership Programme Empowering Young African Women to Drive Social Change appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Youth Service Capacity-Building Grants 2026: NYC Nonprofits Can Receive $60,000 to Strengthen Youth Programs and Organizational Capacity
summary: Small nonprofit organizations serving young people across New York City have a new opportunity to strengthen their internal systems, improve organizational stability, and expand their ability to create positive outcomes for youth. The Youth Service Capacity-Building Grants (YSCG) 2026 program is now accepting applications from eligible youth-serving nonprofit organizations in New York City. The initiative provides funding of up to $60,000 per organization to support projects designed to improve organizational infrastructure and long-term capacity. The program focuses on helping small nonprofit organizations build stronger foundations so they can better serve young people between the ages of 5 and 25 and address inequalities affecting youth outcomes throughout New York City. The application deadline is September 17, 2026, at 3:00 PM EST . About the Youth Service Capacity-Building Grants Program The Youth Service Capacity-Building Grants program supports nonprofit organizations that provide direct services to young people and need additional resources to strengthen their operational systems. Rather than funding individual programs or direct service expansion, the grant focuses on improving the organization behind those services. The goal is to help youth-serving nonprofits become more stable, effective, and sustainable. The program recognizes that many small nonprofits face challenges related to infrastructure, management, technology, fundraising, and organizational development. By investing in these areas, the program aims to help organizations improve their ability to support youth over the long term. The long-term goal of YSCG is to build stronger youth-serving organizations that can contribute to reducing inequality in youth outcomes across New York City. Grant Benefits and Funding Amount Selected organizations will receive: $60,000 in grant funding Flexible support to address organizational capacity-building needs Three years of funding support beginning March 1 Technical assistance and organizational development support throughout the grant period The funding distribution includes: First year: $30,000 Supports launching and implementing capacity-building activities. Second year: $20,000 Supports continued development and implementation of organizational improvements. Third year: $10,000 Requires the organization to secure an additional $10,000 in matching funds from new funding partners. The program encourages nonprofit organizations to use the grant as a foundation for building relationships with additional supporters and expanding their financial sustainability. Approximately three new Youth Service Capacity-Building Grants are expected to be awarded annually . What Types of Projects Can the Grant Support? Organizations may use funding to strengthen areas of their operations that have been identified through formal or informal assessments. Examples of eligible capacity-building activities include: Financial management improvements Board recruitment and board development Human resources management Staff training and professional development Fundraising systems and strategies Strategic planning Information technology improvements Leadership development Communication systems Evaluation and measurement systems Successful applications should clearly explain how improving these areas will strengthen the organization and ultimately improve services and outcomes for young people. Additional Support Provided to Grant Recipients Beyond financial support, selected organizations will participate in technical assistance activities designed to help them achieve their capacity-building goals. The program partners with the Community Resource Exchange to provide: Free one-on-one organizational support Customized assistance based on each nonprofit’s needs Learning community meetings Peer-based learning opportunities Coaching and collaboration opportunities These resources allow participating organizations to share experiences, discuss challenges, receive guidance, and develop sustainable solutions. Eligibility Requirements To qualify for the Youth Service Capacity-Building Grants, organizations must meet specific requirements. Eligible applicants must: 1. Be a Youth-Serving Community-Based Nonprofit Organizations must: Be based in one of New York City’s five boroughs. Provide direct services to young people. Not be a national or international organization. Have staff members who interact directly with youth during service delivery. 2. Serve Young People Ages 5–25 Applicants must serve youth within the required age range. At least 80% of youth participating in services must be between ages 5 and 25 . 3. Meet Nonprofit Status Requirements Organizations must either: Have their own 501(c)(3) tax-exempt status, or Operate under a fiscal sponsor that is a registered 501(c)(3) organization. Organizations applying through fiscal sponsorship must provide confirmation of their sponsorship agreement and relevant documentation. 4. Meet Financial Requirements The applying organization must: Have an operating budget between $250,000 and $1 million during the prior year (2024). Provide audited financial statements or certified financial statements. Reducing Inequality Eligibility Criteria The Youth Service Capacity-Building Grants program focuses on organizations addressing inequality in youth outcomes. Applicants must meet at least one of the following criteria: Serve Youth in High-Risk Community Districts Organizations may qualify if they provide youth services in communities identified as having high levels of risk to child well-being. Eligible districts include: Bronx Community District 1 – Mott Haven Community District 2 – Hunts Point Community District 3 – Morrisania Community District 4 – Concourse/Highbridge Community District 5 – University Heights Community District 6 – East Tremont Community District 7 – Bedford Park Community District 9 – Union Port/Soundview Brooklyn Community District 5 – East New York Community District 16 – Brownsville Manhattan Community District 11 – East Harlem Organizations do not need to have their headquarters located in these districts. However, the youth services being improved must currently operate in one of the identified areas. Serve Specific Youth Populations Organizations may also qualify if they have established programs specifically designed for: Mexican-descent youth LGBTQ+ youth Programs must be intentionally designed for these populations rather than serving them incidentally. Organizations That Are Not Eligible The program does not support: Projects focused only on improving direct program delivery Capital construction projects Scholarships Endowments Lobbying activities Real estate purchases Awards to individuals Public or private schools National or international organizations Organizations primarily serving adults Organizations previously funded through the Foundation’s Youth Service Grant programs must wait at least 18 months after the end of their award period before applying again. Application Review Criteria Applications are reviewed annually by a volunteer committee composed of Foundation staff members with expertise in areas such as finance, communications, and nonprofit management. Applicants should ensure their proposals clearly explain: Organizational Mission and Youth Services Strong applications should describe: The organization’s mission Current youth programs How services create meaningful experiences and positive outcomes for young people Capacity-Building Needs Applicants should demonstrate: They have assessed their organizational challenges They understand the causes of those challenges They can explain how these challenges affect organizational effectiveness Capacity-Building Plan Applicants should provide: A clear plan for addressing identified challenges Specific goals and activities Key staff members or consultants involved A responsible person overseeing implementation Methods for measuring success A sustainability plan for maintaining improvements Important Deadline Application deadline: September 17, 2026 Time: 3:00 PM EST Organizations interested in applying should begin preparing their application materials early, including financial documents, organizational information, and capacity-building plans. How to Apply Eligible organizations can access the application portal and required templates through the official program page. Click here for more and to apply Click here for more global grant opportunities The post Youth Service Capacity-Building Grants 2026: NYC Nonprofits Can Receive $60,000 to Strengthen Youth Programs and Organizational Capacity appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: UNDP Digital, AI and Innovation Internship 2026: Paid Global Remote Internship for Students and Recent Graduates
summary: The United Nations Development Programme (UNDP) has officially launched its Digital, AI and Innovation Internship: Global Call for 2026 , inviting talented students and recent graduates from around the world to contribute to cutting-edge initiatives shaping the future of digital transformation and sustainable development. This fully home-based internship offers participants the opportunity to work with UNDP s Digital, AI and Innovation (DAI) Hub , supporting global projects focused on artificial intelligence, digital public infrastructure, innovation, data governance, product development, policy research, partnerships, and digital transformation. Interns will collaborate with international experts while helping UNDP accelerate progress toward the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) through innovative and responsible use of digital technologies. About the UNDP Digital, AI and Innovation Internship The United Nations Development Programme serves as the development agency of the United Nations, operating in more than 170 countries and territories . As global challenges become increasingly interconnected, UNDP is investing heavily in digital transformation, artificial intelligence, innovation, and data-driven solutions that strengthen governance, improve public services, and accelerate sustainable development. The Digital, AI and Innovation (DAI) Hub acts as UNDP s central platform for advancing: Artificial Intelligence Digital Transformation Digital Public Infrastructure Systems Innovation Data Governance Digital Product Design Capacity Development Innovation for Sustainable Development Interns will become part of this global effort by supporting real-world initiatives that improve lives across developing and emerging economies. Internship Benefits Successful interns will receive numerous professional development opportunities, including: Monthly Stipend UNDP provides interns with a monthly stipend . The stipend amount varies depending on the internship duty location. Since this internship is home-based, payments follow UNDP s internship policy. If an intern already receives financial support from another institution or government, UNDP may pay the difference between that support and the applicable UNDP stipend. Fully Remote Work Experience The internship is entirely home-based, allowing participants to contribute from anywhere in the world while collaborating with international teams. International Exposure Interns gain experience working with one of the world s leading international development organizations. Participants collaborate with experts working across: Digital transformation AI policy Innovation Climate action Governance Sustainable development International partnerships Professional Development Interns develop practical skills in: Research Policy analysis Data analytics Artificial intelligence Digital product management Communications Project management Stakeholder engagement Knowledge management Internship Areas Applicants may support one or more of the following workstreams. 1. Systems Transformation and Innovation Responsibilities include: Researching innovation policies. Conducting horizon scanning. Supporting systems transformation. Designing innovative programme approaches. Producing knowledge products. 2. Programme Delivery and Capacity Building Interns may: Support AI and digital initiatives. Develop learning materials. Organize webinars. Prepare implementation toolkits. Coordinate national digital strategies. 3. Data Policy and Governance Tasks include: Researching data governance. Supporting national data strategies. Preparing policy briefs. Monitoring international digital governance developments. Supporting advocacy on digital rights. 4. Data Analytics and Insights Responsibilities include: Cleaning and analysing datasets. Building dashboards. Monitoring project performance. Producing reports. Translating data into actionable insights. 5. Partnerships and Strategic Communications Interns may: Research technology partners. Draft partnership proposals. Prepare presentations and pitch decks. Develop communication materials. Support social media campaigns. Assist with speeches and global meetings. 6. Process Efficiency and Resource Management Tasks include: Monitoring project workplans. Developing internal guidance documents. Improving operational processes. Supporting procurement and HR planning. Tracking project milestones. 7. Product Design, Development and AI Tools Interns will help: Design digital platforms. Support software development. Conduct user testing. Assist implementation teams. Develop AI-powered digital products. 8. Research and Knowledge Management Responsibilities include: Managing knowledge platforms. Conducting desk research. Producing briefing notes. Managing newsletters. Curating digital knowledge repositories. Skills UNDP Is Looking For Successful applicants should demonstrate: Strong communication skills. Creativity and innovation. Research ability. Adaptability. Teamwork. Problem-solving skills. Attention to detail. Ability to work independently. Positive attitude. Ability to multitask. Additional desirable skills include: Database management. Administrative experience. Project coordination. Digital literacy. Analytical thinking. Required Documents Applicants must submit: A brief cover letter in English. A current CV in English. An application through the UNDP Careers portal. A completed Airtable application form. Important: Both the UNDP application and the Airtable form must be completed for the application to be considered. Internship Duration Contract Length: 3 months Schedule: Full-time Location: Home Based (Remote) The internship start date is flexible and depends on organizational needs. Recruitment Timeline Applications are reviewed on a rolling basis. Successful candidates may hear back anytime between April and the end of 2026 , depending on recruitment needs. Application Deadline Applications Close: September 30, 2026 (The UNDP careers portal displays an application closing time corresponding to October 1, 2026, 03:59 AM UTC , reflecting time-zone differences.) Applicants are encouraged to apply well before the deadline because applications are reviewed on a rolling basis. Why You Should Apply This internship offers a unique opportunity to: Gain UN experience. Build expertise in AI and digital transformation. Work remotely with global experts. Strengthen your international professional network. Contribute to achieving the Sustainable Development Goals. Develop highly sought-after digital and policy skills. Enhance your career prospects in international development. How to Apply To complete your application: Prepare your English CV. Write a brief English cover letter. Submit your application through the UNDP Careers portal. Complete the required Airtable application form. Submit both applications before the deadline. APPLY HERE Conclusion The UNDP Digital, AI and Innovation Internship 2026 is an exceptional opportunity for students and recent graduates seeking international experience in artificial intelligence, digital transformation, innovation, and sustainable development. With a monthly stipend, flexible remote work, and exposure to global development initiatives, the internship provides valuable professional experience while allowing participants to contribute to impactful projects that support countries around the world. If you are passionate about technology, innovation, and creating solutions for global challenges, this internship is an opportunity you should not miss. For more information on this internship opportunity, click here.
